item.title: The best recruiters in sales & trading aren’t who you expect them to be
item.description:
Who are the best (Ideal, even) recruiters to work with in finance? It’s hard to pin down. Professionals can easily consult the eFinancialCareers Ideal Employer Report </ideal-employer> to know the best firms to work for – but what are the best recruiters that can get you jobs at those employers?
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
It’s a good question to ask. And it’s why we’ve launched our Ideal Recruiter rankings, which are based on a survey of over 7,500 financial services professionals that we held across the entirety of 2025.
In the field of sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank>, the ideal recruiter was voted to be Selby Jenning. Selby Jennings also swept the investment banking category </news/ideal-recruiter-banking>.
One respondent to our survey, based in the US, said that the firm was a “pretty good outfit”, which covered “major aspects of the recruitment process which, in my experience, are beneficial to applicants.” He rated the firm top marks on its ability to provide market knowledge and access high-quality people.
In second place was sales & trading specialist recruiter H.W. Anderson. The firm has six key practice areas: Commodities, Equities, Quant Strategies, Macro, Credit and Private Equity, and some fifty employees servicing those functions. It has offices in New York, Edinburgh, Dublin, and Dubai.
Armstrong International came third. The London-based firm, which also has offices in New York, Miami, and Skopje (Macedonia), hires for a variety of industries including hedge funds – and hires in specialties fixed income, macro, credit, and systematic funds.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs' most humble partner on internships to avoid, or not. Ex-hedge fund manager says that most people can never be traders
item.description:
Back when Kunal Shah <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-partner-kunal-shah-international-ai-tech-middle-east-2026-4> was an intern in the early 2000s, people apparently told him “Don't rotate into fixed income trading desks — it's all going to get automated”. Now, looking back on that advice from his position as global co-head of FICC at Goldman Sachs (and co-CEO of the international business), he has a fine perspective on why this was wrong, and what that means for people starting their careers today.
Basically, as Shah points out, it was kind of true that many of the tasks which used to be given to juniors have been automated. Physical trade tickets don’t need to be collected, fewer things need to be typed into spreadsheets and travel can be booked online. But, as is often said, that has merely meant that front-office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking> jobs have become more interesting and really more meaningful for analysts and associates </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>, and so fixed income trading is quite the opposite of a dead end.
Shah believes that today’s generation of interns </news/internship-investment-bank>are well placed to understand that the integration of AI into the job will likely have a similar effect. If building spreadsheet models can be automated, and the font sizes on Powerpoint presentations can be automatically “pls fixed”, that’s going to be great news for people who are able to take advantage of it. As he says, “Junior talent are inherently tech-savvy, and they don't have the legacy of why we do things in a certain way. They can see how to disrupt us.”
Shah himself has gained a reputation over the years </news/2014/11/kunal-shah-goldman-sachs-md-30-partner-32> for being approachable, humble and quite witty </news/kunal-shah-goldman-sachs>.
He is also known to be accessible to junior ranks, reproducing the mentorship and help that he got in the early years. In his most recent interview, he recalls having a coffee with Ashok Varadhan, currently Goldman’s co-head of global banking and markets, back in 2003 when Shah was a fresh analyst and Varadhan was already a partner.
It’s perhaps a little bit unrealistic to hope to follow the career trajectory of someone who’s famous for making Partner at the absurdly young age of 31, but it’s certainly possible to learn from his attitude. If you’re capable of saying that the best thing about your job is that “no day is the same” and meaning it (in reality, lots of them are pretty similar), then you’re well on the way to having it made, and if nothing else, your colleagues are more likely to like you.
Elsewhere, Hugh Hendry is less smooth and easy going. Hendry once evocatively summed up his investment style <https://www.thetimes.com/money/saving-investing/article/i-lost-80m-lessons-you-can-learn-from-a-share-crash-56t5qdr6h> by talking about a climber who had to saw his arm off when it got trapped under a boulder. In his view, it’s understandable that the climber waited a few hours before making the decision, but if you procrastinate like that in financial markets, “you’re going to be found dead on the side of the mountain”.
Many things have changed since then, but Hendry’s philosophy apparently hasn’t. In advertising the “Acid Capitalist Summer Camp <https://hughhendry.substack.com/p/e59?r=3aqks&utm_campaign=post&utm_medium=web&timestamp=7.4&triedRedirect=true>” that he’s promoting in the Caribbean paradise where he now lives, he’s promising to train people how to be more impulsive and not to miss opportunities. As he says, “you watch your own money sit still while everything around it moves higher and you tell yourself you’re being sensible. you wait for it to feel clear. you wait for someone to confirm it. and when that moment comes it’s already gone. you wait until it feels safe. and safe is always after the move.”
But, unlike a lot of investment gurus, he is quite clear that learning this lesson won’t make you into a star trader. In his view, “that takes years. real years. obsessive years. like training for a sport. every day. every hour … that’s fantasy”.
So, rather than learning an investment approach, you’ll just be more likely to saw your arm off if it needs sawing. Which, to be honest, isn’t necessarily the worst use of your investment conference budget for the year.
Meanwhile …
Western AI models are banned in China and now Goldman Sachs bankers in Hong Kong can't access Claude. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/aa3a7a19-ab94-4069-aea4-e192ab9abc41?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
While everyone else in both the private equity buyside and financial sponsors sellside is still talking up the prospects for an IPO boom and lucrative exits, former Goldman Sachs banker Vinay Kumar is putting a dampener on things. As he’s raising a private credit fund for Silver Rock, the firm spun out of Michael Milken’s family office, he’s pointed out that “The era of buying something, levering it up, then taking dividends and selling it at an elevated multiple is over”. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/4bd56a80-948c-4749-a3ce-4cb1ae4ddfc5?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
As the proverb has it, targets are an opinion, forecasts are an opinion but real estate is a fact. Jane Street employees would do well to take note of the fact that the firm has made an offer to roughly double its London office space. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-28/jane-street-set-to-double-footprint-in-london-office-move?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260428&utm_campaign=author_19908303>)
As well as complicating its own journey toward IPO, the fact that OpenAI has missed some revenue targets is likely to be greeted with a sigh by capital markets bankers who are currently working on the SpaceX deal, as xAI is a material part of the valuation there too. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/tech/ai/openai-misses-key-revenue-user-targets-in-high-stakes-sprint-toward-ipo-94a95273?st=WDYNC1&reflink=desktopwebshare_permalink>)
It seems that prediction markets are even better hunting grounds for algorithmic trading than crypto markets. According to recently published research, 69% of all traders lose money, while the top 1%, most of whom show evidence of being bots, account for 75% of all the betting profits made. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-28/most-prediction-market-traders-are-losing-money-while-bots-rack-up-gains>)
Citadel has received regulatory approval to set up its 18th office in a place even sunnier than Miami; they will open doors in the Dubai International Financial Centre. (Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/hedge-fund-giant-citadel-launches-in-dubai-c632f44b>)
NeeDoh, a sort of squashy stress toy, has become the latest TikTok craze, meaning that, inevitably, rare versions are selling for silly money. It’s a bit like NFTs except slightly more dignified. (WSJ <https://www.wsj.com/business/retail/its-a-stress-relief-toy-that-became-a-status-symboland-its-maker-cant-keep-up-fb6b09d8?mod=hp_featst_pos3>)
Sam Bankman-Fried seems to have annoyed the judge in his motion for a retrial. (Citation Needed <https://www.citationneeded.news/sbf-new-trial-denied/>)
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Deutsche Bank needs more new bankers, and its traders are working harder for less
item.description:
It's Deutsche Bank Q1 results day, and maybe Barclays bankers and traders can now feel ok. Yesterday, Barclays </news/barclays-q1-traders> revealed some lacklustre results in fixed income trading but some fine results in M&A </news/get-a-job-in-ma> banking. Today, Deutsche Bank reveals some lacklustre results in fixed income trading and some poor results in M&A banking.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Deutsche Bank's M&A bankers have been misfiring for a while. The German bank has a lot of newish people. Between 2023 and 2024, it hired around 240 senior bankers, of whom many were managing directors. It might be expected that these new bankers would bring new revenues, and yet this hasn't happened yet. Last year, Deutsche's M&A revenues were up only 1%. In the first quarter of 2026, they fell by 13% year-on-year. By comparison, the chart below shows what's happened to M&A revenues at other banks to report so far:
What's occurred in M&A at Deutsche Bank? It might be a question of deal mix, again. Last year we suggested </news/deutsche-bank-bankers> that Deutsche's bankers are strongest in areas like industrials and business services, which weren't the sectors of the moment. In the first quarter of 2026, M&A revenues were driven by big ticket M&A deals in the US, which is an area in which DB is still developing. </news/deutsche-bank-s-investor-day> Either way, Deutsche remains patient with its hundreds of new hires whose novelty may now be wearing thin: it said today that no money was spent on restructuring in the first quarter.
Beyond M&A advisory, Deutsche Bank's equity capital markets (ECM) bankers performed best in Q1 (up 27%), but its fixed income salespeople and traders seemed to suffer in the same way as those at Barclays and Goldman Sachs. </news/barclays-q1-traders>- Deutsche said its fixed income trading revenues fell year-on-year "due to reduced revenues in rates." DB added that its FX trading revenues rose and neglected to mention its performance in credit.
Although revenues at Deutsche Bank's all-important fixed income trading division fell compared to the prior year's record quarter, its traders were hard at work. The German bank said today that fixed income client activity was up 8% in the first quarter against a "strong prior year."
For the first time, Deutsche also split out the currencies in which its revenues and costs are denominated. The chart below suggests it might be a fine thing for Deutsche Bank if the value of GBP fell substantially relative to the dollar or euro. In the past quarter, though, DB said FX headwinds impacted revenues in the investment bank.
For the moment, then, Deutsche Bank's traders especially seem to be working harder for less. The bank cut compensation spending by 3% year-on-year in the first quarter, while increasing headcount by 3% over the same period.
Deutsche Bank declined respond to a request to comment.
Costs and revenues by currency at DB:
Source: Deutsche Bank

item.title: Banks are technology firms. So why aren't they cutting jobs tens of thousands of jobs too?
item.description:
It's been a while, but anyone who still remembers 2015 may also remember that Lloyd Blankfein, then the CEO of Goldman Sachs, famously declared <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-has-more-engineers-than-facebook-2015-4> that Goldman Sachs was a technology company rather than a bank. At that time, Goldman Sachs had 9,000 engineers among its 33,000 employees. This made its engineering team larger than Facebook's, said Blankfein.
Ten years on, and Goldman Sachs' headcount was 47,000 people at the end of March. Goldman doesn't volunteer its current technology headcount, but it's thought to be over 12,000 people. JPMorgan employs another 63,000 technologists <https://www.aol.com/news/jpmorgan-engineers-efficiency-jumps-much-190410172.html>. Citi has 60,000. <https://jobs.citi.com/job/belfast/senior-engineer-vice-president/287/90544880368#:~:text=We%20are%20an%20expert%20team,who%20significantly%20impact%20the%20bank.>Even HSBC has over 30,000 <https://www.ventures.hsbc.com/view/traditional-banks-are-becoming-technology-incubators#:~:text=Banks%20as%20large%2Dscale%20technology%20users&text=The%20scale%20and%20complexity%20of,technology%20is%20tested%20at%20scale.> people in its tech team.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Facebook's headcount has also grown. In 2015, it employed only 13,000 people. Two months, ago Meta's headcount was closer to 80,000. But last week, Facebook announced plans to cut 10% of its workforce and to cancel 6,000 planned new hires as it invests in AI. It's not alone: Oracle, <https://www.cnbc.com/2026/03/31/oracle-layoffs-ai-spending.html>Microsoft, Amazon, Dell, Block and others are doing the same. Blind, <https://www.teamblind.com/> the forum which used to be full of FAANG engineers discussing high pay is now full of engineers complaining of their dystopian futures. <https://www.teamblind.com/post/meta-is-dead-and-depressing-37frzvw7>
If technology firms are cutting tens of thousands of staff as they go hard on AI, why aren't banks doing the same?
The answer depends who you ask. "The bloodbath will come," claims one senior technologist at JPMorgan, speaking off the record. "It will just take longer because the banks are slower."
As we reported last week, </news/jp-morgan-ai-naughty-list>JPMorgan has begun tracking how often its developers use its suite of AI tools and has categories for "non," "light" and "heavy" users. JPMorgan says these are simply so that it can understand how well-used the tools are; insiders there are not so sure.
"When banks finish rolling out Claude Code or Codex to the majority of their techies, the cuts will come," the JPMorgan engineer tells us. "I think we will start to see cuts in Q4 as bonus season approaches. Individuals and teams will become surplus to requirement."
Some banks are anecdotally more gung-ho with AI coding tools than others. Goldman Sachs is rumoured to be among the circumspect, but insiders at the firm say technologists are still likely to suffer from the firm's rolling rounds <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-layoffs-cuts-underperformers-sra-spring-summer-2026-3>of job cuts </news/goldman-sachs-immense-quarter-was-accompanied-by-400-job-cuts>in 2026. One senior engineer there says underperformers have been added to an exit list. A spokesperson for Goldman Sachs said: "As always, Goldman Sachs is focused on supporting our people through our talent management, development and engagement efforts. We do not comment on speculation or rumours.”
While banks are trimming technology headcount, though, they are not making the kinds of wholesale cuts seen in big tech. The senior Goldman engineer, also speaking off the record, says this is because banks have yet to see sufficient benefits coming from AI to enable them to replace people. "At the moment, banks have just halted incoming headcount instead," he says.
Another senior technologist at a US bank confirms the nil-hiring trend. "Hiring is on hold while we explore the fabled 20% productivity boost from AI," he says. "The emphasis is on reallocating existing staff rather hiring new ones."
Will big cuts come later? He's not so sure. "Banks have to be careful because they're regulated and there's money at stake," he reflects. "The recent problems at Amazon where AI-written code resulted in thousands of client orders being cancelled have scared banks. They're not going to cut. They're just not going to hire."
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Quant poker: The new path to a job at an electronic trading firm
item.description:
There are plenty of ways </news/how-important-are-spring-weeks-and-insight-events-when-applying-for-quant-trading-internships> to show off your risk appetite and technical skills to electronic trading </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> firms like Jane Street. </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> Few of them are as good a conversation starter as winning a poker tournament without placing a single bet yourself.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Fullhouse Hackathon, <https://fullhousehackathon.com/>which describes itself as "the UK's first quantitative poker hackathon," is currently open for registration. The competition, which takes place in June, is sponsored by five major electronic trading firms: Jane Street, Teza, Five Rings, Quadrature and Susquehanna. Participants have to create a bot in Python that will automatically bid, raise and bluff depending on the level of risk.
There's a £4,000 prize pool but perhaps the more valuable prize on offer is "office tours at top quant firms," presumably the offices of the sponsors. It also promises "career introductions."
Poker runs deep in the DNA of these trading firms. Jeff Yass funded Susquehanna <https://www.forbes.com/sites/antoinegara/2021/04/06/how-trader-jeff-yass-parlayed-poker-and-horse-racing-bets-into-a-12-billion-fortune/> using money he earned from poker, and the Wall Street Journal <https://www.wsj.com/finance/investing/poker-wall-street-trading-options-stocks-4223c308> reported in 2024 that interns there spend at least 10 hours a week playing poker. Jane Street, which spun out of Susquehanna, employed female poker legend Vanessa Selbst </news/2023/09/vanessa-selbst-jane-street> as of 2023. Michael Lewis' book on Sam Bankman-Fried </news/2023/10/jane-street-interview-process-sam-bankman-fried>said that the firm also used a modified version of poker in its internship interview process. Less is known about Five Rings </news/a-quant-trading-firm-paying-806k-in-london-works-from-a-flexible-workspace>, but it spun out of Jane Street; the love of poker appears to run in the family.
Professional poker players on Reddit <https://www.reddit.com/r/poker/comments/109gdtt/anyone_here_play_poker_fulltime_whats_your/> say that "making $10k-20k in a month is doable and repeatable. You can earn far more than that at these trading firms. Jane Street paid over $2m on average to its employees in 2025 while the most recent pay figures at Quadrature </news/quadrature-pay-london> suggest that the average pay there is £2.8m ($3.8m)

item.title: Blackstone's head of APAC recruiting has defected to Goldman Sachs
item.description:
It's not just bankers who have exit opportunities in private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity>; the recruiters that hire them do as well. Vikas Shetty took his exit a year ago when he joined Blackstone </news/do-you-still-need-an-investment-banking-graduate-job-to-get-hired-by-blackstone>in Hong Kong </news/this-is-what-your-private-banking-salary-and-bonus-should-be-in-hong-kong>but, just 16 months later, he's back in banking.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Shetty has joined Goldman Sachs in Hong Kong as an MD and head of recruiting for Goldman Sachs Asia Pacific. He was most recently head of APAC talent acquisition for Blackstone. The firm has ramped up its hiring in the region in recent years; Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-01-26/blackstone-eyes-more-hiring-in-asia-to-tap-private-market-demand> reported in January that APAC headcount in its private wealth team has doubled over the past two years.
Shetty previously worked in recruiting across various banks including Credit Suisse and Deutsche Bank. His last role before Blackstone was at JPMorgan, where he was APAC lead for front office recruiting.
In addition to bankers, Shetty helped grow the asset management teams in his previous banks. Goldman will presumably be happy about this; Goldman Sachs Asset Management </news/goldman-sachs-wealth-management-jobs>has been lauded as one of the fastest growing divisions in the bank by CEO David Solomon.
Goldman and Blackstone are both rising fast in Asia. A study from Broadridge <https://fundselectorasia.com/blackstone-and-goldman-sachs-climb-rankings-in-apac-fund-brand-survey/> found that both firms were in the top 10 asset management firms in Asia in 2026. Blackstone rose three places to seventh while Goldman Sachs entered the top 10 at 9th.
Goldman Sachs did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Boutique bank PJT hired more dealmakers than juniors this quarter
item.description:
America’s big banks posted their results earlier this month, and now it’s time for America’s boutique banks </news/boutique-investment-banks>. The first line, which was released today, was Paul Taubman’s boutique, PJT Partners.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
PJT did well, notching a record quarter in both revenue and pretax income. More notably, the firm added eight new partners across Q1, going from 133 to 141. The new partners were all in either strategic advisory (M&A </news/what-are-ma-jobs>) or restructuring </news/2023/05/investment-bank-restructuring>. Those numbers do not account for departures, but it means that those two factions make up 79% of PJT’s partners – Park Hill, its private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> arm, did not gain any new partners.
The number of total employees was not up so dramatically. From 1,224 at the start of the quarter to 1,229 at the end. Given the increase in partners, it’s very possible that PJT’s non-partner headcount declined by a percent throughout the quarter.
It’s likely that headcount will continue to grow. CEO and founder Paul Taubman noted that the firm was “well positioned to thrive,” and gushed over its “future growth prospects.”
Pay was a high point for PJT, too. The firm’s total compensation bill for the quarter shot up by 27%, and due to the flat headcount, average pay per head rose from $194k to $228k. The bank could be on track to pay $1.1m on average by the end of the year, depending on how things go.
As usual, the messianic, zealous praise of PJT’s employees was part of its quarterly disclosure. “The humility of the people here,” one employee was employed to reminisce. “For a firm full of such impressive, high caliber individuals, it was amazing to see how the humility of our people makes everyone constantly strive to be better, learn, grow and support our culture.” Presumably the employee who said that also considers themselves impressive, high caliber, and humble.

item.title: Jane Street's interview "gauntlet" is going viral
item.description:
Electronic trading firm </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> Jane Street </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street> is one of the most notoriously difficult places to get into, not least because of its rigorous hiring process. In the wake of its record-breaking year, various people who failed their Jane Street interviews </news/2023/10/jane-street-interview-process-sam-bankman-fried> have gone viral for revealing the questions that stumped them.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Deedy Das, <https://x.com/deedydas/status/2047885377768484994> a partner at VC firm Menlo Ventures, started the trend. In a post that has gathered 8.9m views, Das claimed that Jane Street puts potential interns through a "gauntlet of mental math." He said that he went through six rounds and was stumped by the following question:
"What is the next day after today in DD/MM/YYYY where all the digits are unique?"
The method is just as important as the answer (if not more) when interviewing at Jane Street. Das claimed that the interviewer offered him a pen and paper to do the calculations, but he "knew that was an instant no." After giving an answer, Das was also asked how sure he was of his answer on a scale of 0-1; the interviewer allegedly laughed when he gave an answer of 0.95. Being overconfident can cost you.
Agustin Lebron <https://x.com/AgustinLebron3/status/2047902543095705788>, a former Jane Street trader, responded to say that he originated the question. He said he "actually stole it from a University Challenge buzz in;" watching the show may now qualify as interview prep.
Following Das' tweet, various people revealed their own tribulations interviewing at Jane Street. Alex Song <https://x.com/alexsongis/status/2047899046489047126?s=20>, a former VP at New York fintech Ramp, claimed that he interviewed there in 2010 while working as a trader for Morgan Stanley. He called it the "worst interview of his life," in which he was given the rules for a card game then told he had an hour to figure out a dominant winning strategy.
Brian Huang, <https://x.com/BrianInCrypto/status/2048443253976944955?s=20> a former trader at rival firm XTX Markets, said he was asked a "maniacal" question by Jane Street while he was at MIT (from 2014 to 2018). The question, responses claimed, is also used for undergraduate math admissions interviews for Cambridge University:
"You are given 30 strings (physical strings, not code strings).
By tying ends of the strings together and using all of the strings, what is the expected number of loops created?
For example, you can tie both ends of one string together to form a loop. If you did this with all of the strings, 30 loops would be created. You can also tie two strings together to form a bigger loop. If you did that with all the strings you’d have 15 loops.
What is the expected number of loops if all of the ends are tied together?"
We can't say for certain whether Jane Street, which did not respond to a request for comment, actually asked these questions. A lot of these anecdotes also took place years, if not decades ago, so the specific questions above could have fallen out of circulation if they are true.
Plenty of responses to Das' tweet came from people who had clearly never interviewed at Jane Street. One joked <https://x.com/FICMBondTrader/status/2048437889067778136> that they were told to "look for a sign" and that they were later approached in a bar and told to count how many blondes were in the room from memory. Such questions, we can safely assume, will not be asked when you interview for Jane Street.
Rather than a gauntlet, Jane Street's website says that your "interview will feel a lot more like a conversation than a quiz." It also says that the math required to answer these problems is no more complicated than "what you might learn in a higher-level math course in college." We've looked at recent interview questions reported online here </news/electronic-trading-interviews>, and analyze Jane Street's mock interview on how to answer questions here </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-jane-street#:~:text=What%20questions%20does%20Jane%20Street%20ask%20in%20interviews%3F>.

item.title: Junior bankers desperate to move to private equity paid for vanished information: "I've lost $300"
item.description:
The greater the need for privileged information, the greater the potential to sell it a profit. Alternative data providers have monetised this. Now private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity>insiders are doing the same.
Two junior bankers in London tell us they have lost money after being approached by a mysterious individual on Telegram and banking forums, <https://www.wallstreetoasis.com/>offering private equity recruiting materials in return for payment in crypto. When they paid him, they say the materials disappeared and so did he.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
"I've been burned," says one of the junior bankers affected. "He contacted me by direct message and offered me case study pdfs and Excel files for interviews with a specialist private equity firm. When I said that the price seemed too high, he also offered me an audio recording of the interviews. I went ahead and he sent me a large audio file which sounded authentic, but as soon as I paid, it disappeared."
Another junior banker described a similar sequence of events. "He demanded that I send the money ($300) before I receive the full materials," he says of the apparent scammer. "When I sent it, he blocked me and removed me from Telegram."
The individual is since understood to have been blocked by the forum concerned, but the junior bankers say he's still active on Telegram. The materials shared, seen by eFinancialCareers, include a case study </news/private-equity-case-study-interview>interview for a content distribution business, in which applicants are given 150 minutes to prepare a financial model predicting the multiple on invested capital (MOIC) and internal rate of return (IRR).
Both the junior bankers we spoke to said the materials shared were drawn from the application process at Apis Partners, a private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> and venture capital </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-venture-capital> firm investing in financial services and technology businesses. Apis declined to comment. The juniors said they were hoping Apis would clamp down on the leak, and possibly even refund their money. However, one said the emails he sends the firm have begun bouncing back.
“The individual sounded professional and clearly did work in finance,” says one of the junior bankers we spoke to. "Private equity recruitment is so tough that the level of detail he was offering would have really helped. The individual claimed that he was part of the recruitment process. I asked why he was willing to risk it for the sake of a modest sum and he said there would be plenty of applicants.”
Selling dubious advice on getting into banking is becoming more common. Last year, we reported </news/fake-jpmorgan-credit-trader> on a trader who purported to work for JPMorgan and was charging students £500 an hour. JPMorgan said they had no record of him working there.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>
Have a confidential story, tip, or comment you’d like to share? Contact: +44 7537 182250 (SMS, Whatsapp or voicemail). Telegram: @SarahButcher. Signal: sarahbutcher.22 Click here to fill in our anonymous form </about/editor-tips>, or email editortips@efinancialcareers.com.

item.title: Pay for risk & compliance professionals keeps falling. Even MDs earn $400k
item.description:
Much has been made about the bonuses that bankers </news/investment-banking-pay-2025> and traders </news/sales-trading-pay> earn, and my, how much they’ve grown. But financial services professionals have one group of colleagues that worked hard in 2025 – and saw their compensation cut rather dramatically.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Our Compensation & Lifestyle Report 2026 </banking-compensation-guide>, which was based on a Q1 survey of over 2,700 financial services professionals, introduced a new reporting category: Finance </news/accounting-job-finance-banking>, Risk </news/risk-job-investment-bank>, Operations </news/operations-jobs-banking> and Compliance </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-compliance> (FROC). FROC covers the major functions of an investment bank’s support staff - the middle office </news/front-office-middle-office-back-office-banking>. It covers professionals who are neither revenue generating nor technologically </news/how-to-get-a-technology-job-bank> focused.
In the FROC category, the salaries and bonuses are lower. And they’re also declining – according to respondents to our survey, total compensation in FROC was down 6% in 2025 compared to 2024, with a significant average salary fall (8% down) leading the charge, and an average bonus decrease of 0.4% adding insult to injury.
Compensation fell across the board for all ranks within FROC. The biggest decline – of nearly 40% - was for managing directors (MDs), who earned a total of $412k last year. “Bonus cut despite good corporate earnings,” said one finance/audit MD, based in Singapore, who responded to our survey. He reported a 20% decline in his bonus.
$412k for MDs sounds like a lot, but is low compared to front office roles such as investment banking or trading. VP traders responding to our survey earned more than that ($465k on average), as did director-level investment bankers ($568k on average).
Still, there was some optimism. Bonuses for associates in FROC were up by 7.8%, which was matched by a fall of 8.5% in salary. VP bonuses only fell by 7% from a low base, while salaries only fell by 1.9%. Director bonuses were up by 1.2%, with salaries down 6%.
Low compensation in FROC roles was driven, above all else, by low bonuses. The average FROC VP in our survey earned a bonus of just $18k, which was 23% of their base salary. The average banking and trading VP earned a bonus of $154k and $201k, which was 70% and 76% of their salaries, respectively.
“Minimal pay increase despite achieving targets and taking on more responsibilities,” said a London-based operations VP for an American bank. He was not satisfied with his pay. “More deliverables, but less compensation,” rued a Hong Kong-based peer working for a French bank.

item.title: Hedge fund QRT's Eisler quant came and went in three short months
item.description:
People from deceased hedge fund Eisler Capital </news/2023/06/eisler-capital-management> are appearing elsewhere. But sometimes they only appear briefly, like a beetle in mating season.
This has occurred in the case of Steffen Hippler, a former systematic commodities trader at Eisler Capital who joined Qube Research and Technologies </news/qrt-hedge-fund> in January 2026 before leaving again a few weeks ago.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Neither Hippler nor Qube responded to a request to comment for this article, but Hippler's short session at Qube is confirmed by the FCA Register, which states that he joined on January 22nd and left on April 2nd.
It's thought that Hippler moved of his own volition. There are suggestions that he has joined Tim Hayes, another former Eisler portfolio manager </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle> who's now at Hartree Partners, a commodities trading firm. Hartree also declined to comment.
Hippler's flight may not have been Qube-induced. At Qube, he was on the systematic digital assets trading team, but Hippler has long been a commodities trader and spent two years at Shell trading European oil and gas before Eisler hired him. At Hartree, he will be trading commodities again. Former Eisler colleagues point out that there is more money to be made in commodities than crypto now.
Hayes has commodities pedigree too. He spent 15 years trading oil at BP before his own stint at Eisler. Hippler may hang around longer with Hayes than with his brief colleagues at Qube.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Anthropic's product team is led by ex-bankers and traders. This is who it's hiring.
item.description:
Many of the shiny jobs at AI labs </news/ai-researcher-pay-culture> like Anthropic </news/ai-hedge-fund-jobs-vs-banking> involve deep researchers in the weeds of AI advances. There's a lot more to it than just that though; the Anthropic lab has a small team of product managers (PMs) led by ex-bankers, traders and VCs. Members say the team is incredibly difficult to hire for.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Catherine Wu, head of product for Claude Code, recently appeared on a podcast <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PplmzlgE0kg> to discuss Anthropic's product </news/product-manager-job-bank> team. She said it has 30-40 product managers currently, and that it will "pretty much hire anyone" with the right product taste. The problem is that taste is "still a very rare skill to have."
Wu said the Anthropic product team has two halves. Product research is “responsible for understanding all of the feedback from our customers for our models and then feeding that to the best research team to act on it." That team is led by Dianne Na Penn; she spent the first four years of her career as a JPMorgan high-yield bond trader </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> following the financial crisis, but went on to work as head of product for LLM modelling in Amazon's Alexa AI team. Wu also worked at JPMorgan briefly, interning in its equity derivatives desk before stints at Scale AI and VC firm Index Ventures.
Wu said the other product team is product growth, "responsible for growing [users] across our entire product suite." That team is led by Amol Avasare; he worked in Macquarie's TMT group as a graduate, but joined Anthropic from fintech </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-fintech> unicorn Mercury.
The function of these PMs is to get users "onto the golden path," and interact with Claude in the most efficient way possible. "We get tens of thousands of GitHub issues asking for every single thing under the sun," Wu said. "It takes a lot of care and taste to figure out which of these is worth building.” She said that, generally, Anthropic hires product managers with engineering backgrounds as they'll have a better idea of how difficult each of those technical problems would be to solve.
There were rumours that product managers were falling out of fashion earlier in the decade. For example, Meta's 2023 'year of efficiency,' </news/2023/03/product-vs-engineering> notably focused on creating a "optimal ratio" of engineers to everything else at the expense of product. AI seems to have changed things; Wu said that "as code becomes much cheaper to write, the thing that becomes more valuable is deciding what to write."
Anthropic's product team also has a "small pod of folks who collaborate very closely" with its deep research people. Things may be different at competitor OpenAI; insiders there previously told us </news/chatgpt-openai-culture> that some of the longest-serving researchers "don't respect the product side as much."

item.title: "Hedge funds are becoming colder, darker, less forgiving"
item.description:
The multimanager hedge fund </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring> machine is still hiring. But the tone around it is no longer euphoric.
The latest signal is Jain Global </news/jain-global-hedge-fund-jobs>. Bobby Jain’s </news/jain-global> hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> firm is reportedly preparing to return external capital and manage money exclusively for Millennium Management </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium>, where Jain was previously co-CIO.
This matters, because Jain Global </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience>was supposed to be one of the defining independent launches of the pod-shop era. Jain had the pedigree, investor attention and platform-building credibility. Less than two years later, the story appears to have shifted from independent expansion to dependence on Millennium's capital.
That is not necessarily failure </news/barclays-q1-traders>. But it is not the original bullish narrative either. The backdrop was already uncomfortable. GIC reportedly <https://www.hedgeweek.com/gic-redeems-jain-global/> redeemed US$250m from Jain Global around 18 months after investing. Jain’s multistrategy fund also reportedly <https://news.bloomberglaw.com/international-trade/jains-multistrategy-hedge-fund-gained-3-7-in-first-full-year> gained only 3.7% in its first full year of trading, modest compared with stronger results from several established multimanager rivals.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The point is bigger than Jain. It is the model. Multi-manager hedge funds still pay </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-pay-millions> well, hire aggressively and pull talent from banks. But they are not easy places to build a career. A widely circulated and unverified post by Paopo Economico last week cited median tenures of just 1.8 years at Point72 and Balyasny, 2.3 years at Millennium and 3.0 years at Citadel. It also claimed that one large fund hires around 160 portfolio managers a year, with annual PM turnover of 15% to 20% treated as a structural feature of the model.
Whether every number is exact or not, the direction feels right. Pod shops are designed for capital efficiency, not career security. Capital is allocated, monitored, cut and redeployed. People are part of that same process. If performance is strong, capital scales quickly. If drawdowns appear, capital can be cut. If losses breach limits, the seat can disappear.
Raising capital is one challenge. Hiring investable teams, building infrastructure, satisfying allocators and creating enough confidence to launch at scale is another. That also makes senior sell-side moves into hedge funds more interesting, but more ambiguous. Hoe Lon Leng </news/now-nomura-s-global-head-of-rates-and-fx-trading-resigned>’s move from Nomura to Modular Asset Management fits the current pattern. He was previously Nomura’s global head of FX flow trading. His departure came after Nomura brought in senior ANZ markets talent </news/nomura-s-singapore-macro-hiring>, changing the leadership structure around the macro trading business.
The sequence matters. This was not simply another senior banker leaving for the buy side in isolation. It also reflected banks reshuffling leadership while hedge funds continue to absorb senior macro talent.
On one level, this is still the familiar story: experienced sell-side traders leaving banks for hedge funds, where upside can be higher and risk-taking can look cleaner. On another level, it shows a more fragile market. The buy side remains attractive, but the hedge fund route is no longer the simple upgrade it once appeared to be.
Banks are bureaucratic. Hedge funds are unforgiving. At banks, senior traders complain about committees, capital limits, balance-sheet constraints, internal politics and compensation structures. At pod shops, the rule is simpler: performance is judged quickly. There is less shelter. A drawdown can reduce capital. A bigger drawdown can end the seat.
Jain Global shows that even a high-profile launch by a former Millennium star can struggle to sustain the independent-platform narrative. It challenges the idea that the hedge fund industry can simply keep absorbing talent, raising capital and scaling new platforms without friction.
The hedge fund industry is not collapsing. The largest platforms still have capital, infrastructure, prestige and hiring power. But the easy-growth narrative has faded. Scale is expensive. Talent is expensive. Technology, data, financing and risk infrastructure are expensive. Investor patience is finite. Even famous founders with elite credentials do not get unlimited time to prove the economics work.
For jobseekers, the message is blunt. Hedge funds are still hiring, but this is not a free option. Platforms want portable alpha, clean attribution, tight risk control and fast monetisation. A senior title at a bank may open the door, but it will not protect anyone once inside.
For banks, the lesson is more nuanced. Senior traders will continue to leave for hedge funds, especially in rates and FX. But not every departure should be treated as permanent damage. Some traders will thrive in the pod-shop model. Others may find that the freedom of the buy side comes with much less institutional protection.
The hedge fund industry still has money and prestige. It still hires aggressively. It still appeals to ambitious traders who want more upside than banks can offer. But the tone has changed. Even at the top end, the hedge fund model is becoming harder, colder and less forgiving.
Follow me on X <https://x.com/MadameButcher>. Follow me on LinkedIn. <https://www.linkedin.com/in/sarahbutcherjournalist/>

item.title: Morning Coffee: Barclays' traders may have made Goldman Sachs' traders' mistake. Jain Global says its Millennium move is really great
item.description:
It's European bank results week. Barclays <https://home.barclays/investor-relations/reports-and-events/financial-results/#latest> is first off the block with a set of results suggesting that if its fixed income traders were to speak to their counterparts at Goldman Sachs, they might find things in common.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As a reminder, Goldman Sachs' traders underperformed their US rivals by virtue of problems on the macro desk </news/goldman-sachs-rates-traders>: Goldman's traders were positioned for interest rates to fall; the war means this hasn't happened. Goldman's fixed income sales and trading revenues fell by nearly 10% as a result.
Did something similar happen at Barclays? The British bank's fixed income trading revenues were up 1% in Q1. This was the second worst performance, after Goldman Sachs, so far.
What went wrong? Barclays doesn't say, but the omission may be telling. While Barclays says its markets revenues were driven higher by equities and by credit trading, there is no mention of macro (rates and FX) trading. By implication, macro traders seem to have been the problem.
For the moment, it's worth remembering that Barclays' macro trading team has lost a lot of good people </news/goldman-sachs-rates-traders> and will probably need to hire some more soon. When it does, it may find Goldman Sachs in the market for talent too.
Separately, now that hedge fund Jain Global </news/jain-global> is giving back investors' money and will soon be taking money entirely from Millennium, </news/jain-global-hedge-fund-jobs>there have been observations <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/jain-global-to-return-cash-exclusively-manage-millennium-money?sref=DK3y4h9m>that maybe it's not so easy to set up your own multistrategy hedge fund </news/multistrategy-hedge-funds-hiring> after all. The rising cost of "talent, infrastructure and capital," is becoming prohibitive, one hedge fund headhunter observes to Bloomberg.
Let this not be said to Bobby Jain, though. Bobby says that taking money from Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> is not capitulation but the opportunity he had hoped for. The Financial Times <https://www.ft.com/content/336c945a-03fd-464e-9201-0b1cea0f4e5c?syn-25a6b1a6=1> reports that Bobby told his people yesterday that: “Scale is paramount in this industry...We always knew that, and we took the challenging route out of the gate to get there. Millennium just collapsed the timeline.”
If this is not convincing enough, Jain also reportedly declared that: “It’s about accelerating our trajectory in a period where scale and resourcing will define the winners and losers,"
Maybe all hedge funds should run on Millennium's money in the future.
Meanwhile...
Goldman Sachs is very excited about M&A and says that although there is "humility" in the market, the cycle has "momentum." Investors are having very deep thoughts about "terminal value," which means the value of the company in the infinite future. "They have to buy terminal value. So acquirers are leaning in. " (Goldman Sachs) <https://www.goldmansachs.com/insights/articles/ma-volume-expected-to-surge-this-year-despite-economic-uncertainty?chl=og&plt=tw&cid=cb&agp=aat&cre=expectedsurge&plc=feed>
Citi hired James Carolan from Deutsche Bank as global head of FX structuring in New York. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/citigroup-hires-deutsches-carolan-to-lead-fx-structuring-2f4f38f8?mod=fngooglenews>
RBC hired Jackson Graham as the head of single-name CDS trading from Squarepoint Capital. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/rbc-hires-graham-boccio-as-heads-of-cds-and-electronic-trading?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260427&utm_campaign=author_20649314>
Investors in Blue Owl Capital Corporation II do not want to sell to Boaz Weinstein for 65 cents on the dollar. Weinstein has been saying things like: “We’re in the super early innings of the wheels coming off the car . . . for this industry of semi-liquid, contingent liquid products facing rising redemptions, which is going to create its own velocity.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/634b8423-277f-437f-a613-7375d56d76dc?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Chak Raghunathan, the former chief risk officer at Apollo, says life insurance businesses owned by big funds could struggle in a downturn. “When you’ve got asset manager owned insurers, who are not traditional life insurance people, you get in trouble, because the liquidity premium that has been assumed away is a significant risk.” (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/0e1ddf60-1987-40ef-b07e-912937ce5cbd?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
London bankers won't be so fit and proper. The UK government is touting a bill that's s expected to allow it to scrap the certification regime, which requires financial services companies to check thousands of senior staff are fit and proper every year and record the results on a central directory. Banks want ringfencing rules relaxed too. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/5811b388-1b45-42c9-b2a3-baa72e7807b8?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
The British govenmet also wants to shorten the time for bringing IPOs to market by rolling back a 2018 rule designed to bring external research into IPOs so that investors get an external perspective on the firms coming to market. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/uk-proposes-cutting-ipo-timetable-by-a-week-to-boost-listings?srnd=homepage-americas>

item.title: JPMorgan traders prefer London: "People didn't want to stay in Paris"
item.description:
JPMorgan moved some traders to Paris and now, it seems, it is moving some back again.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/jpmorgan-moves-some-paris-traders-to-london-in-brexit-rethink?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260427&utm_campaign=author_21123532> reported today that the US bank is relocating a "handful" of roles back from Paris to London, particularly in its rates trading team. The US bank reportedly over-estimated the number of staff it would need in Paris to comply with EU rules, and its traders themselves wanted to return.
JPMorgan declined to comment for this article, but one senior trader at the bank told us the relocations to London are nothing new and have been occurring for some time. "It's been a slow transition to be honest," he says. "A lot of people didn't want to stay in Paris. They've moved back slowly over the years."
Quite how many people have moved back to London is a source of dispute. JPMorgan is understood to have 1,000 people currently located in its Paris offices </news/jpmorgan-paris-offices>, up from 250 around the time of Brexit. A new 16,000 square foot office is due to open at 37 Place du Marché Saint-Honoré next year, and JPMorgan has said its sales and trading teams will be among the first to move in.
However, some of JPMorgan's Paris trading teams are thought to have leaked a lot of traders to London with the result that they're now skewed towards sales. One senior trader tells us JPMorgan isn't alone in this. "In many cases, the traders in Paris are just placebos. All the real risk is taken in London," he claims. In the early days of Brexit, EU regulators warned banks against operating "letterbox operations </news/2020/04/banks-hiring-frankfurt-covid-19-brexit>" in Paris and Frankfurt while risk was directed back to London using so-called mirror trades. Now they seem less bothered.
Bloomberg says JPMorgan's traders are leaving Paris in part for tax reasons. As we reported in March </news/bankers-in-paris-are-ready-to-return-to-london-as-tax-deals-run-out>, the tax advantages offered by France's "inpatriates" tax regime last only eight years. For many traders who moved after Brexit, those years are nearly up.
While traders in Paris are moving back to London, JPMorgan is also hiring French traders from rival banks in the City. Last month, for example, Guillaume Bioche, the head of government bonds algo trading at BNP Paribas, joined JPMorgan's London rates electronic trading team. Bioche is, clearly, French.

item.title: Millennium quietly hired the CTO of a major HFT firm to work on nanosecond latency architecture
item.description:
We've noted </news/hft-jobs-medium-frequency-trading> in the past that 'high-frequency trading' (HFT) firms </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> are branching out and doing much more than just their namesake. They've been building out teams with hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> talent, but the flow of talent isn't one way. Hedge fund Millennium, </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> for example, quietly brought in the chief technology officer of one of the world's largest HFTs earlier this year.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Alexandre Fournier joined Millennium in February. It's not clear what exactly his title is (Millennium did not respond to a request for comment), but indexed job listings on google show him as the hiring manager for an open role in the fund's 'systemic platform execution and exchange data' (SPEED) team. Job listings say the SPEED team works on data architecture "across latency tiers from sub100 nanoseconds to millisecond sensitive workflows."
Fournier was out of the market on gardening leave for the entirety of last year, having departed Tower in December. According to his FINRA <https://files.brokercheck.finra.org/individual/individual_5280785.pdf> profile, he spent nine years officially holding the CTO title, but was most recently its global head of architecture Before Tower, he spent 11 years at SocGen, latterly as its head of IT for high frequency trading.
Although there's a belief that hedge funds and trading firms have varying cultures, Tower bears an interesting similarity to Millennium; they're both pod shops </news/2023/10/citadel-millennium-hedge-funds#:~:text=Pod%20shops%3B%20autonomy%20and%20centralization%C2%A0%C2%A0%C2%A0>. At Tower, Fournier concurrently held a technology officer title within 'Latour Trading LLC', a subsidiary home to one of its largest pods. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/tower-research-recruiting-quants-sma-deals-2025-11> reported last year that Tower was offering similar agreements to the 'seperately managed accounts' Millennium often gives top traders who want to run their own fund.
Fournier's new team at Millennium is currently hiring for a feature framework engineer. The description says Millennium is looking for a "best in class" C++ engineer, and is offering a salary of up to $250k.

item.title: What now for jobs at hedge fund Jain Global?
item.description:
Jain Global </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience>, the hedge fund run by former Credit Suisse </news/2023/04/bobby-jain-credit-suisse-millennium-hedge-fund> trader and Millennium </news/how-to-get-a-job-at-hedge-fund-millennium> co-chief investment officer Bobby Jain, is undergoing a change of direction. Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-27/jain-global-to-return-cash-exclusively-manage-millennium-money> reports that Jain Global will be returning all external cash and managing money exclusively for Millennium, starting from October 2026.
Jain Global and Millennium declined to comment for this article. The change of approach has raised eyebrows over the outlook for Jain Global's employees.
Bloomberg reports that Jain Global employs 200 investment professionals plus 200 other staff globally. The firm's recently filed ADV form suggests that it actually has 427 full time employees, of whom 240 are in investment advisory functions including research.
It might seem that the 187 people not working in investment advisory roles are those whose jobs are most at risk from the Millennium tie-up. But this is emphatically not so. An internal memo sent by Millennium to its employees and seen by eFinancialCareers states that Jain brings "complementary talent," and adds that, "Jain Global will remain an independent firm, retaining its own investment processes, operating model and talent base." The memo also says that Jain Global will gain access to Millennium's "infrastructure," but this is understood to refer to vendors and tools rather than to Millennium's human beings. Bobby Jain himself is understood to have personally reassured employees that they are wanted. "To be clear, this is not about subsuming us and extracting synergies. It is all about accelerating our trajectory in a period where scaling and resourcing will define the winners and losers," he informed them today.
It's expected that Jain will remain an independent brand, placing it in a situation not dissimilar to WorldQuant. The implication is that Jain's portfolio managers and analysts will be retained under the new set-up. Jain's platform specialists, working across data, technology and other functions may be replicated at Millennium, but Jain's comment suggests he will be keeping all these people too. Whether Jain will still need external fundraising expertise seems questionable. "There will be a lot of overlap in some functions," observes one headhunter.
Bobby Jain will continue to run Jain Global. Speaking in February, Bobby said he liked to hire "35-year-old-killers," who simply need someone to back them in order to perform. Bobby also said that Jain Global hadn't hired "hundreds" of its own tech people, because it had been able to create its contemporary systems with the help of AI. Maybe Jain Global doesn't have many tech people anyway.
Jain Global is different to Millennium to the extent that it runs an "integrated" model rather than independent trading pods. Jain Global also seems to have a more lenient approach to drawdowns. Bobby Jain said in February that he likes to discuss where trades have gone wrong with his PMs. By comparison, Millennium is known to operate a strict quantitative approach that typically results in capital allocations being halved when 5% is lost and to portfolio managers losing their jobs when losses hit 7.5%.
Millennium added that the two firms have "highly aligned approaches to capital allocation and risk management." It said that Jain Global "represents a natural extension of our strategy to partner with proven investment talent to accelerate and amplify their success."
Jain Global trades 61 products and runs seven different strategies (fundamental, quantitative and arbitrage in the equities business; rates, macro and credit in fixed income; plus Asia) across three regions (EMEA, Americas, APAC) and six offices (New York, Houston, Greenwich, London, Hong Kong, Singapore). It's thought that this "seven legged stool" approach was part of Jain Global's appeal, implying that Millennium has no intention of shutting any of the strategies down.
Questions were raised previously <https://rupakghose.substack.com/p/jain-global-getting-past-the-j-curve?has_completed_unsubscribed_unlock=true>about Jain Global's comparatively low AUM per employee. The firm currently has $6bn under management. Presumably Millennium will at least match this, although its commitment is not clear.
Bobby Jain said in February that his firm had been built with operating leverage in mind and that assets there could triple without Jain Global needing to hire many new people. Maybe this is what Millennium will now enable? If so, Jain Global's top performing portfolio managers who receive larger capital allocations could be the real beneficiaries of the coming change.

item.title: The best recruiters in investment banking - ranked
item.description:
Working out the best financial services firms to work at is easy – a professional simply needs to consult the eFinancialCareers Ideal Employer Report </ideal-employer>. But getting into an Ideal Employer is easier said than done. You might ask yourself which headhunters are best to work with. Luckily, the eFinancialCareers Ideal Recruiter Report can help you do that.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
The 2026 Ideal Recruiter report is based on a survey we ran across 2025, which received over 7,500 responses from industry professionals. Each one was asked their industry – and each one was asked what the best recruiter in their space was.
One of the industries our respondents came from was investment banking </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process> – and, by quite some margin, the most popular recruiter in investment banking was Selby Jennings. The firm, which has offices in the US, London, Amsterdam, Berlin, Zurich, Singapore, Hong Kong, and Shanghai, is the marquee brand of global recruitment agency Phaidon International.
In second place was Dartmouth Partners. Headquartered in London and with offices in New York and Charlotte, the firm was founded in 2012 by former PwC analyst Logan Naidu. It’s now headed up by Terri Loska, who worked with Naidu at recruitment firm the Cornell Partnership, a firm he also founded.
Dartmouth “supported me throughout my interview process with insight into the field and the firm,” said one London-based private equity </news/how-to-get-a-job-in-private-equity> professional in our survey. “They have positioned themselves to be visible to the professionals of the field with excellent industry networking,” said another.
Maven Partnership came third in our ranking of banking recruiters. The firm – which has more than 80 people – with offices in New York, West Palm Beach, London, Dubai, and Hong Kong. Maven was founded in 2007 by Gaurav Sethi, and it is now headed up by Will Hannaford as CEO, with Rizwaan Ahmed serving as Managing Partner for Global Banking.

item.title: Yes, Citadel Securities made some advance internship offers to Harvard students
item.description:
Achieving an internship at Citadel Securities </news/2023/07/citadel-interns> is not easy. Last year, the firm accepted just 0.4% of the tens of thousands of students who applied. This year, the firm's intern acceptance rate is likely to be lower, again.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
As Citadel Securities </news/2022/10/how-to-get-a-job-at-citadel> sifts through its mountain of intern applicants, some students are being fast-tracked. Last month, we understand that the firm met directly with students at Harvard University, and extended internship offers to them as a result.
Citadel Securities declined to comment for this article. The firm's Harvard fast track has been the subject of various complaints on X <https://x.com/WestsideLAGuy/status/2044631895124947117>. There have been suggestions that students received expedited offers following meetings with Citadel Securities' CEO Peng Zhao and other Citadel Securities executives.
Speaking at the Semafor Conference <https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xnGe1m9sMs4> earlier this month, Citadel Securities' chief people officer Alex DiLeonardo said the firm has a "culture of meritocracy" and "real objectivity" around managing talent. As technical skills become more commoditized, candidates are being assessed for broader characteristics like creativity, leadership potential, commerciality "raw problem solving ability," DiLeonardo added.
Citadel Securities' internships usually start in June and last for around 11 weeks. The firm typically uses phone screening followed my multiple in-person and technical interviews. Interns earn $24k a month and usually spend part of the internship staying at a deluxe hotel.
Harvard students who didn't receive offers during the meeting with Zhao and other executives are still in Citadel Securities' standard recruitment process and may still receive offers.

item.title: Boutique bank Rothschild hired a new chief data officer in London
item.description:
Boutiques </news/boutique-investment-banks>, unlike their major investment banking counterparts, aren't known for hiring thousands of technologists. </news/career-paths-technology-banking> They keep their tech teams lean like the rest of their organization. At Rothschild, the tech team appears to be expanding as adopting AI becomes increasingly important.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Rothschild has hired Kiran Gill as a group chief data officer, operating within the group data and digital office. It appears to be a new role; Rothschild did not respond to a request for comment, but we can't see any prior chief data officer. Paris-based Emmanuelle Saudeau has been chief digital officer since 2020, presumably working within the same team. Gill says on her LinkedIn that the role will involve utilizing data effectively and enabling AI. </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>
Gill joined after a four month stint at pension fund L&G working on data transformation. She spent eight years prior to that working in senior data roles across insurance firms QBE and Lloyds of London. Earlier in her career, she was also head of data quality and governance for the UK's National Health Service.
Although boutiques are small already, their executives think they can be smaller. Lazard MD Peter Orszag has previously said <https://www.ft.com/content/5cf20f55-bf7c-40de-8b88-50b01c048ffc?syn-25a6b1a6=1> that he expects deal teams to shrink as his bank continues to adopt AI. Similar things may be in the works at Rothschild.

item.title: Boutique bank Evercore hired a JPMorgan industrials banker, promoted him to MD
item.description:
If you’re an investment banker </news/the-investment-banking-recruitment-process>, there’s one thing to be, and one place to be: to be a managing director </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> and to be at a boutique bank. Sometimes – if you’re pretty lucky – you can get both at once.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Allen Shalam joined Evercore last week as a managing director, based in the firm’s New York office. Shalam joined the bank from JPMorgan, where he spent 11 years – and was lastly an executive director in its business services coverage team.
When a boutique bank like Evercore hires a managing director, things are rarely as simple as they seem. An MD at Evercore is not quite the same as other places – the firm also employs “Senior” Managing Directors, similar to the “partners” employed by firms such as PJT or PWP. These are the real revenue generators at the firm.
Evercore’s Q1 results haven’t been released yet, but its Q4 (and full year 2025) results showed that it hired generously across the entire year – around 200 people in total, from a starting headcount of 2,380. It seems that the firm’s appetite for people has continued to develop.

item.title: Nomura's Singapore macro hiring is still going on
item.description:
When Nomura announced its fourth quarter results </news/nomura-2026-results>last week, its fixed income sales and trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> revenues were not the best. They rose by just 2% last year and the bank said its macro revenues declined in the Americas, at least.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
The next time Nomura reports quarterly revenues, the situation may have changed. The Japanese bank is spending heavily to build out its macro business. Not in America, but in Singapore.
Nomura isn't commenting, but another raft of macro hires in Singapore are understood to be on their way. Multiple sources inform us that Yagnish Rathi, a former Morgan Stanley FX trader who spent the past two years at hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job> Verition is joining Nomura's Singapore office. So too, we understand, is Ryan Fong, the head of financial sponsors clients FX sales for Asia at ANZ. Neither Rathi nor Fong have confirmed their moves.
We also understand that Anshul Sidher, the former global head of markets at ANZ, is arriving at Nomura in Singapore today.
The new arrivals are the latest in a stream of hires that also include Herr Ling Yeo </news/nomura-s-massive-commodities-hire-arrived-in-singapore-in-the-nick-of-time>, ANZ's former global head of FX and commodities (who arrived last week), and Raghav Joshi </news/nomura-made-another-big-hire-for-its-singapore-macro-team>, a senior G10 FX trader who arrived from Santander but who also previously worked for ANZ.

item.title: Traders enjoyed big bonus increases after a strong 2025
item.description:
While much attention has been given to the strong performance of investment banking </news/investment-banking-pay-2025> teams across 2025, sales & trading </news/how-do-you-get-a-job-in-sales-and-trading-in-a-bank> teams have also had a strong year – according to market intelligence provider Tricumen, global equities revenue increased by 18%, and global fixed income, currencies, and commodities revenue increased by 12%.
Click here to join the bubble by eFinancialCareers, our new anonymous community. <https://bubble.efinancialcareers.com/login?_gl=1*ut8n6u*_gcl_au*MTg4MDY2NTExNi4xNzYxNTU3NDIz*_ga*ODczNzI2NTA0LjE3NTM2OTk2ODI.*_ga_NZFQV4MXPM*czE3NjM2NDM0MDYkbzI1NiRnMSR0MTc2MzY0MzQzOSRqMjckbDAkaDMyMjc4OTUzMw..>✍️
Data from our Compensation & Lifestyle Report 2026 </banking-compensation-guide> suggests that salespeople and traders were well-rewarded for this. Theirs was the best paid banking function globally, and on average salaries and bonuses increased by 9% and 17%, respectively. Total compensation, therefore, was up by 13%.
The highest bonus increases were for mid-level professionals: associates and Vice Presidents </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds> (VPs). Average bonuses for these went up by 24% each, approximately, to $85k and $201k, respectively. Associate total compensation only increased modestly (5.4% up), however, on account of a 5.1% decline in salaries. VP salaries increased by 17%.
The lowest bonus increases, of just 8%, were for analysts in sales and trading. However, on this floor of the pyramid, bonuses make up a relatively small proportion of total compensation, and salaries – the more significant part – went up by 31%.
Managing Directors (MDs) bonuses were up significantly, by 19%. Matched with a more modest salary increase of 7.8%, total compensation for senior salespeople and traders reached $837k in 2025, an increase of 12.5% on their total compensation in 2024.
