item.title: Morning Coffee: Some ECM bankers are getting 30% pay rises. Goldman Sachs' CEO made a joke
item.description:
Of all the product lines in an investment bank, IPOs are possibly the one where the personal franchise of specific employees matters the most. Even if a bank executes dozens of transactions every quarter, each one of those is one of the most important days in the history of the corporate client. An IPO is a unique, one-time deal; other equity and debt capital markets business and even M&A advisory can be spread about and new banks tried, but the IPO always goes to the bankers that a client trusts the most.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
So, relationships matter. Most of all, the relationships between the “rainmaker” coverage bankers and the CEOs, founders and key investors who make the decision about lead roles. But it’s also important to have a good reputation in the wider industry, which is what gets you into the lower down – but still lucrative – positions in the underwriting syndicate. And that doesn’t just go for the big names and managing directors; people put their trust in the entire team, including all the ranks right down to vice presidents and even associates </news/banks-weird-hierarchies-analysts-associates-vps-mds>, that everyone will do their job, documents will be produced accurately and to deadline, and that nobody will do anything that jeopardises the deal. When there is an IPO boom going on, there is hardly any amount of money that’s too much to pay for someone who can keep your franchise respectable.
In the Indian market at present <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-21/banks-dangle-30-raises-for-dealmakers-to-meet-india-ipo-demand?srnd=phx-deals&embedded-checkout=true>, we are seeing a particularly extreme version of this labour market effect. The Indian IPO market is on course for a third consecutive record year. This is attracting both new entrants and increased interest from the global bulge bracket. The incumbent and domestic banks are, naturally, keen to invest previous years’ profits in preserving their market share. All of which means that the Indian equity capital markets industry wants to create an estimated 300 new jobs, according to recruiters like Sheffield Howarth and Native.
That is quite a lot of headcount to source in a fairly limited talent pool. It is possible to transfer employees from other geographies, promote internally and even raid neighbouring industries like law firms. But the banks are likely to want to do a lot of poaching from one another, in order to get candidates with that vital local experience and reputation.
And the only way to do that is to pay up. At present, local headhunters suggest that bankers moving from one firm to another are realising “raise on move” increments of as much as 25-30% of compensation. This is likely to mean that bid-back payments are in the same order of magnitude, and that local banks will be raising comp for people who don’t move, just in order to keep them happy.
In circumstances like this, banks quickly remember the importance of keeping a loyal team and a strong culture, because it is much easier to retain people if they’re not feeling grumpy and unloved in the first place. What a pity that they don’t always remember this fact when the market turns back down.
Elsewhere, unlike his predecessor Lloyd Blankfein, Goldman Sachs CEO is not what you’d call an instinctive comedian. He has a fair line in amusing anecdotes (like this one <https://www.tiktok.com/@wallstreetoasis/video/7518062147851570488> about changing the GS dress code), and has been known to play a decent straight-man <https://www.businessinsider.com/goldman-sachs-david-solomon-could-signal-big-culture-changes-ahead-2018-3> to more flamboyant presenters. But in terms of snappy one-liners, he’s usually been a bit more cautious – his wise-cracks have been more on the level of telling an analyst <https://www.euromoney.com/article/27bjsstsqxhkmh1znxfp6/opinion/goldman-held-an-investor-day-and-there-were-jokes/> at an investor day that they can come to dinner because they said something nice about Goldman.
So it’s important to recognise a witticism from Solomon when one comes along. When he said that the US economy was “only one tweet away <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-21/solomon-says-us-recession-risk-could-be-only-one-tweet-away?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260421&utm_campaign=author_21835441>” from the risk of recession, he just joking, says Goldman spokesperson, David Fratto. Presumably everyone is laughing.
Meanwhile …
Fintech banker Jeff Gido was nearly lured away from Goldman Sachs in 2018, close to the peak of the last fintech boom. It possibly reflects the state of the tech and financial cycles that he has now been promoted to be co-Chair of the global financial institutions group. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-21/goldman-names-fintech-banker-gido-co-chair-of-financials-group?srnd=homepage-europe&embedded-checkout=true>)
The “brutal war for talent” that Anthropic have apparently started for AI programmers in London is certainly a step up for the industry. With salary offers around $750k, people will have their heads turned. (Techloy <https://www.techloy.com/anthropics-630k-london-salary-sparks-brutal-talent-war-for-tech-startups/>)
MBaer Merchant Bank was launched to take advantage of the big Swiss banks’ increased risk aversion after some big US regulatory decisions ten years ago. But the trouble with being less risk averse than the competition is, well, the risk. And after some big US regulatory trouble of its own, it’s now closed. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/861bbb39-9cdb-4e80-933a-46b0d7104e5e?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
It’s too early to say whether the Iran conflict is as much of an “ill wind that blows some good” as the Russian invasion of Ukraine was, but so far it appears that the majority of specialist commodity traders have got on the right side of most of the big trends, and have navigated between the tweets and Truths to make profits at least comparable to the 2022 bonanza. (Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-21/commodity-traders-reap-billions-in-profit-as-war-upends-markets?embedded-checkout=true>)
Lawyers in the restructuring practice of Sullivan & Cromwell are apparently in for some “enhancements” to their training process, after a senior partner found himself in a court apologising for a filing that contained more AI hallucinations than you’d ideally like to find in something that had cost thousands of dollars per billable hour. (FT <https://www.ft.com/content/657d86df-5e0d-4d03-bf0c-cb768a58e758?syn-25a6b1a6=1>)
Some reviewers of the video game “Crimson Desert” found that the medieval combat mechanics weren’t as exciting as the potential to pretend to be a hedge fund manager while investing your spare gold. (Khaleej Times <https://www.khaleejtimes.com/entertainment/gaming/crimson-desert-video-game-review-after-100-hours>)

item.title: Jane Street's AI jobs have more going for them than $300k salaries
item.description:
Jane Street, the electronic trading firm </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms> with fingers in many pies, likes hires to machine learning engineers and AI researchers. And they, in turn, should like to work for Jane Street.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
It's not just the pay, which is predictably generous </news/jane-street-entry-level-pay> at $300k in salaries alone for machine learning engineers and researchers in New York. It's also the compute, which Jane Street has a lot of.
In AI terms, compute ("computational power") refers both to hardware and to infrastructure like GPUs that enable a system to perform calculations. Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/ai-compute-compensation-software-engineers-greg-brockman-2026-3>reported last month that access to compute has become a critical consideration for AI engineers choosing wear to work. At Jane Street, they will be rich in this metric. And they will get richer.
A year ago, we suggested that Jane Street might have 5,000 GPUs </news/why-some-jane-street-traders-are-sloppy-data-scientists>. Since then, things seem to have picked up a bit. A current vacancy for a machine learning researcher, says Jane Street has "tens of thousands" of high end GPUs and a "rapidly growing computing cluster."
Jane Street's cluster includes access to NVIDIA's next-generation Vera Rubin technology. The firm secured access to this last month after investing $1bn in Coreweave, an AI-native cloud computing platform, and spending a further $6bn to access Coreweave's services. Jane Street also has investments in Claude-creator Anthropic, and although the electronic trading firm wasn't mentioned <https://www.anthropic.com/news/claude-opus-4-7> among the lucky places that got to test Claude Opus 4.7 last week (only Balyasny appeared to receive this perk among financial services firms), it's unlikely to be ignorant of the new model either.
For AI engineers who care about such things, Jane Street's growing level of compute puts it among the market leaders. XTX Markets also likes to boast about GPUs, and has 25,000 of them and 650 petabytes of usable storage according to its current job advertisements.
Like Jane Street, XTX is moving to secure its computational future. Last year, XTX announced that it was building five data centres in Finland, costing $1bn. They're due to be completed in 2026.
XTX also pays well. And it has a fake space ship in its London office. </news/2023/03/algorithmic-trading-office>Both matter, but maybe GPUs matter more nowadays.

item.title: Who Kalshi, Polymarket and more are hiring as prediction markets grow
item.description:
Prediction markets </news/electronic-market-makers-trading-on-kalshi-are-small-fish-in-a-big-pond>, in which people bet on the likelihood of real-world events via event contracts, have exploded in popularity over the past few years. As institutional players like Citadel Securities </news/hedge-fund-gardening-leave#:~:text=It%20sounds%20like%20Citadel%20Securities%20will%20want%20to%20hire%20prediction%20markets%20specialists%20soon%2C%20but%20not%20for%20sports%20contracts.%20%E2%80%9CWe%E2%80%99re%20not%20there%20yet.%20There%20is%20not%20that%20much%20liquidity%2C%E2%80%9D%20says%20president%20Jim%20Esposito.%20As%20markets%20%22ramp%20and%20scale%22%2C%20the%20firm%20will%20%22potentially%20get%20involved.%E2%80%9D> are becoming increasingly interested, these firms are starting to ramp up their growth efforts... but who are they hiring?
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
For one, prediction markets firms are in the market for staff found at those hedge funds and trading firms interested in trading on it. Peter Wagner, an MD at quant headhunting firm Affinity North, called Kalshi "a new player in the market for elite talent" in a market report last month. Polymarket, meanwhile has mentioned trading firms in multiple job listings; it asked for prop trading experience in an opening for a trade surveillance analyst, and for HFT </news/high-frequency-trading-hiring-and-pay> experience when recruiting for a senior backend engineer. In an opening for a senior C++ </news/finance/c-coding-language-problems> engineer, it also asks for specialists in low latency techniques common in hedge funds </news/low-latency-c> like lock-free programming </news/the-expert-c-programming-technique-you-will-need-to-know-for-a-hft-interview> and multithreading.
There are many hedge fund alums working in these companies already. Last month, Polymarket hired Mukilan Ashok as its interim US CTO; he was most recently global head of electronic trading engineering for credit at Citadel. Kalshi co-founder Tarek Mansour, meanwhile, previously interned at Citadel. </news/tarek-mansour-kalshi-sam-bankman-fried-ftx>
Kalshi is also hiring traders on salaries of up to $250k for an affiliated trading entity that provides liquidity to the platform. They're expected to have a consistent PnL on Kalshi, trade large volumes on the platform, or have a strong background in traditional finance, sports betting </news/quant-sports-betting-jobs>, or competitive games like poker </news/2023/09/vanessa-selbst-jane-street>. Polymarket was reported to be building a similar team as of December. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-04/polymarket-builds-in-house-trading-team-as-it-re-enters-the-us>
Polymarket, more so than Kalshi, is looking for crypto </news/why-i-will-never-work-in-crypto-ever-again> and web.3 enthusiasts. It uses smart contracts written in Solidity </news/2022/07/solidity-developer-jobs>, a language built on the ethereum blockchain. Various blockchain technologies are mentioned across its job listings; it helps to have experience in roll-up technologies like Arbitrum Nova, as well as byzantine fault tolerant (BFT) consensus engines. Blockchain specialists are likely to become of increasing importance at Kalshi, which announced in December <https://news.kalshi.com/p/kalshi-solana-tokenized-predictions> that it was tokenizing some of its markets through Solana.
Both teams are building out new teams and functions right now. Polymarket says that is has "no existing QA infrastructure," and is hiring a quality assurance engineer to build a team. It's also hiring its first technical program manager. Kalshi, meanwhile, has an open listing for its first product manager, likening the role to a founder (although several people have joined as product managers recently).
These firms are still startups, so naturally most of the people joining them are arriving from other startups and scaleups. Dan Lee joined Polymarket's product team from Coinbase, where he spent over four years; Lee was also previously an associate director at electronic trading firm Tower Research. Software Engineer Shen Jin joined last month from Scale AI, but he also spent three years with Robinhood.
These teams are still very much in their infancy. In a podcast last month, Kalshi's founders said the fintech has just 120 employees. A now-closed job listing <https://www.builtinnyc.com/job/business-operations-lead/8587561>for Polymarket said it had 110 people at the start of this year but would be scaling to 260 people by the end of 2026.
As with any exciting new technology, incumbent players are trying to get involved. The biggest fintechs building out prediction markets are Robinhood and Coinbase. The latter entered the space by acquired The Clearing Company <https://www.coinbase.com/en-gb/blog/Coinbase-to-acquire-The-Clearing-Company-Powering-the-future-of-prediction-markets>, a prediction markets platform led by multiple Polymarket and Kalshi alums including ex-Polymarket head of engineering Liam Kovatch and head of growth Toni Gemayel. Robinhood initially launched its platform in partnership with Kalshi but, earlier this year, it teamed with Susquehanna to acquire MIAXdx <https://robinhood.com/us/en/newsroom/robinhood-prediction-markets-joint-venture/>, a derivatives exchange that was once upon a time owned by Sam Bankman-Fried's FTX. </news/2023/11/sam-bankman-fried-ftx-fallout>

item.title: SMBC is making some big macro hires in NYC and promoting to MD in the process
item.description:
If you're looking for a new macro trading job in New York, SMBC may be a good place upon which to cast your eyes. It's hired at least two big macro traders there this month and there are rumours of one more in the pipeline.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
SMBC's latest recruit, Shrikar Shah, joins from Morgan Stanley as head of interest rates structured trading. Shah spent nearly 17 years at Morgan Stanley and was an executive director there. He joins SMBC as a managing director (MD) and has combined his new job with a promotion.
Shah's boss at SMBC is Gagan Sobti. He also joined this month, arriving from Bank of America, where he was head of USD interest rates volatility. At SMBC, Sobti is head of all macro rates trading.
There are multiple unconfirmed rumours that SMBC has another big hire from a European bank in the pipeline, but he is thought to be working his notice period.
SMBC's US markets business is run by Masahito Nonaka, a Japanese bond salesman who joined Nikko Securities in Tokyo in 1993 and who has occupied his current role since 2024.
Last month, there were suggestions that SMBC might mount a takeover of Jefferies </news/jefferies-smbc>. However, they appear to have been premature.

item.title: JPMorgan building out a new data analytics division in Singapore under Deloitte partner
item.description:
JPMorgan </news/back-to-office-jpmorgan>'s quest to adopt AI </news/how-to-get-a-banking-ai-job>has been relentless in the last few years, but now it's increasingly focused on doing so in Asia. It just brought in a new chief analytics officer who will be building out a data analytics team in the region.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Gautam Gorki </news/2023/09/dbs-to-big-four>started work at JPMorgan in Singapore this week, joining from big four firm Deloitte, where he was head of data and AI for financials services institutions in Southeast Asia. Prior to that, he was an executive director at Singaporean bank DBS, where he created and led its analytics centre of excellence; the bank has been using AI to cut up to 4,000 contract workers </news/banking-contractor-jobs-singapore-hong-kong> since the start of last year.
JPMorgan's data team in APAC today is thought to be focused more on data governance and management, in which teams ensure its quality and manage it like an asset. Gorki's new team will be focused on embedding agentic and generative AI into JPMorgan's Asian businesses using that data. The team will initially be based in Singapore, but could expand further in the future due to the heavily nuanced nature of APAC markets.
JPMorgan's AI efforts are currently focused in the US, where it has made multiple other senior hires already this year. It hired Ronald Jansen, </news/jp-morgan-hired-the-head-of-ubs-ai-lab-to-assess-employee-performance> head of UBS' AI lab, in February, and hired AI scientist Lambert Mathias </news/jp-morgan-hired-an-ai-scientist-from-meta-s-reality-labs-after-its-vr-head-quietly-left> from Meta's Reality Labs last month.
JPMorgan did not respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Where hedge fund Citadel's portfolio managers are mostly found now (not Miami)
item.description:
If you work for hedge fund Citadel, there are certain advantages to being based in Miami. </news/citadel-miami> Ken Griffin lives there and he is spending $2.5bn building <https://floridiandevelopment.com/work-starts-on-citadels-2-5-billion-miami-headquarters/> a 54 storey new Citadel headquarters in the southern city. Taxes are low, temperatures are high and the beach is near.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
So far, though, Citadel's US investment staff seem to be hanging out elsewhere. They are mostly in New York City instead.
Citadel's recently filed annual ADV form for 2025 highlights this state of affairs. The fund now employs 576 investment professionals in New York City. In Miami, it employs just 21.
Nor has Miami's quotient of Citadel investment professionals risen in the past year as Chicago's has fallen. Measured in these terms, Miami does not appear to have benefited from Chicago's recent shrivelling, although Chicago staff are thought to have moved in the past.
Investment professionals include both analysts and portfolio managers. In combination, Citadel and Citadel Securities employ nearly 400 people in Miami. To the extent that these people are working for the hedge fund rather than for the market maker, they are presumably in technology and other support roles.
Things might change when the 54 storey new Miami office is completed in six years time. For the moment, thought, portfolio managers appear to prefer to the facilities at 660 Park Avenue.
Outside of New York, the ADV filing is a reminder that Citadel's second biggest investment office is in London, where staff numbers dwarf the Miami-sized office in Paris. It's also a reminder that Citadel's second biggest European office is now in Hamburg, where the firm inherited staff after it bought power trader FlexPower last October. <https://www.ft.com/content/516a4d69-00e9-4813-a53e-59cd1a3dedc8>
Citadel declined to comment.

item.title: Hedge fund Jain Global is hiring quants in London
item.description:
Bobby Jain's hedge fund </news/how-to-get-a-hedge-fund-job>, Jain Global </news/hedge-fund-jain-global-hired-a-new-portfolio-manager-who-sounds-really-perfect-for-it>, is in the habit of hiring '35-year-old killers'. It's also in the habit of hiring quants; </news/quant-jobs-finance>the fund has brought in multiple quants in recent months with a particular focus on London.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Paul Woodward joined Jain in London this month as a senior quantitative developer after three years at Citadel. Woodward has completed the trifecta of quant roles already; he was a quant researcher </news/what-is-a-quant-researcher-hedge-fund> at ExodusPoint before joining Citadel, and a quant strategist at Morgan Stanley before that.
It also hired Alexander Kazantsev as a senior quant researcher. He's a Russia-educated </news/best-university-for-quant-finance-career>PhD mathematician who previously worked at WorldQuant which, like Jain Global, has roots at hedge fund Millennium. Most recently, he was a quant research analyst at hedge fund Graham Capital.
Jain Global's quant leadership appears to be concentrated in London. The city is home to Anton Merluskin, head of quant modelling and analytics for Jain. He's another Russia-educated quant who spent a collective 25 years at Credit Suisse across two stints, separated by a short spell at ill-fated hedge fund Eisler Capital. Peter Bolland, a former Morgan Stanley MD and CIO for quantitative strategies at Jain, is also London-based.
Jain is still a very young hedge fund. 2025 was only its first full year of trading, in which it posted modest returns of ~3.7%. Bobby Jain said on a podcast earlier this year </news/as-jain-global-p-ms-take-headhunter-calls-bobby-jain-explains-why-they-need-patience#:~:text=%22As%20you%20get%20more%20data...as%20you%20get%20more%20experience%2C%20that%20all%20gets%20better%20and%20it%20gets%20better%20every%20day%20and%20the%20teams%20get%20more%20comfortable%20with%20each%20other%20every%20day.%20And%20I%20feel%20that%20palpably.%22> that, alongside experience, more data will improve things; bolstering its quant ranks may help Jain Global put said data to good use.

item.title: Nomura's massive commodities and FX hire arrived in Singapore in the nick of time
item.description:
Herr Ling Yeo is back in the game, and the game has never been more complicated.
Ling Yeo has just arrived at Nomura in Singapore, where he is a managing director in the Singapore office. Sources at the bank say he manifested himself there today.
Ling Yeo has been on gardening leave for the past three months after leaving ANZ </news/there-have-been-some-exits-at-smbc-and-anz-in-asia>in January, and has therefore missed the volatility created by war in the Middle East. We reported that he was probably joining Nomura in February. </news/nomura-is-thought-to-be-hiring-anz-s-senior-escapees-in-singapore>
Ling Yeo was head of global FX and commodities at ANZ. Nomura doesn't have a commodities operation and he is thought to be replacing Hoe Lon Leng, the bank's former head of FX and flow rates trading, who left in February.
Now that Ling Yeo is on the floor at Nomura, the expectation is that his friend and former colleague, Anshu Sidher, the former global head of markets at ANZ, will turn up too.
Nomura didn't respond to a request to comment.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Goldman Sachs MD says bankers must intrepidly meet clients despite war. Deloitte's two-tier benefit system strikes dud note
item.description:
If you work in investment banking </news/what-is-an-investment-bank> and were hoping that war in the Middle East and resulting high aviation fuel prices might be the catalyst for a return to remote meetings, you are probably not a very good banker. You are also wrong. War in the Middle East is the catalyst for meeting clients more than ever. Clients need reassurance. They also need face-to-face persuasion to do deals.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
With this in mind, Anthony Gutman one of Goldman Sachs </news/how-to-get-a-job-goldman-sachs>' most senior bankers in London, has been in Dubai and Abu Dubai recently. Speaking to local publication the National, <https://www.thenationalnews.com/business/2026/04/20/i-got-stuck-in-traffic-its-a-healthy-sign-top-goldman-sachs-banker-says-uae-bouncing-back/> Gutman declared, "We're in the relationship business. These relationships are no different to any other relationships in your life. And when you have a friend in need, you come and see them.”
Gutman's arrival comes as the British government is advising against <https://www.gov.uk/foreign-travel-advice/united-arab-emirates> all but essential travel to the United Arab Emirates and cautioning that travel insurance might be invalidated if this advice is ignored. Gutman presumably doesn't mind if he loses his suitcase. “Look, I went for lunch today. The restaurant was full. I went for a coffee at Starbucks. I had to queue up to get my coffee," he said. Even being stuck in "incredible traffic" was a positive. "It's clear that the perception of what's going on here versus the reality is different," enthused Gutman, who runs Goldman's the London office with Kunal Shah </news/kunal-shah-goldman-sachs> and was once a hotels and leisure banker by trade.
Gutman was not in Dubai to sample the Frappucinos. He was there to feed the pipeline of deals. "Tailwinds" are still blowing, said Gutman. The pause will not last. The advisory backlog is close to record levels and Middle East deals will be strong this year, albeit a bit backdated. Now is the time to grease the wheels.
It's not just Gutman who feels the breeze. Goldman CEO David Solomon extolled the "robust" climate for M&A earlier this month, and claimed that geopolitical risks are being "trumped" by clients' urge to do "consolidating trades" and to merge themselves with appropriate others.
Gutman said Goldman's other bankers are eager to get on the road too. The firm's teams are "consistently" choosing to return, Gutman said. Being on the ground is a necessity. Dispensing platitudes behind a screen will not get deals done.
Separately, as AI threatens to take over jobs that don't involve drinking Starbucks with concerned clients, Deloitte seems to have decided to bifurcate its workforce and to offer poorer benefits to humans occupying the sorts of roles at risk of automation.
Business Insider <https://www.businessinsider.com/deloitte-zoom-are-shrinking-popular-benefits-will-others-follow-2026-4>reports that the Big Four </news/working-for-the-big-four-pwc-deloitte-ey-kpmg>firm has begun reducing parental leave for people in support roles like administrative services, information technology, and finance and that it may also cut back paid time off, pensions and IVF funding for people in these groups. AI does not need any of these things.
Meanwhile...
Morgan Stanley paid its top London bankers £1.2m last year. (Financial News) <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/morgan-stanley-hikes-uk-investment-bank-bonuses-to-1-2m-4f0d2de3>
Sebastian Barrack, head of global commodities at Citadel, says he has a screen purely to monitor Trump's tweets. “You need to understand that the market is moving [based on] this information,” even though, “it may not be fully thought through”. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/23b14d1b-8efa-4346-ab06-30db101e7748?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Alexander DiLeonardo, chief people officer at Citadel Securities, says technical skills are being commoditized to an extent. "Creativity, leadership potential, raw problem-solving ability, commerciality," are all the rage instead. (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/citadel-chief-people-officer-sjoerd-gehring-exits-hedge-fund-2026-4>
Pity the private credit salespeople. They are working harder than ever, for less pay. "You're stuck saying, 'Buy more please.'" (Business Insider) <https://www.businessinsider.com/private-credit-sales-fundraising-stress-redemptions-2026-4>
Stefan Ericsson at Taula Capital has gone to Jersey. Last year, Taula hired Lewis Morton from Eisler who is based in Jersey too. (Financial News) </news/lewis-morton-eisler>
Andrea Orcel's hostile takeover of Commerzbank became more hostile after he declared Commerz “a story of operating underperformance” yesterday. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-21/unicredit-s-pursuit-of-commerzbank-reaches-decisive-phase?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260421&utm_campaign=author_3407732>
Former UK politician Chuka Umunna is leading JPMorgan's expanded initiative to invest in industries critical to national defence in the UK and Europe as well as in the US. The bank plans to hire in the UK and Europe to support the initiative. (Financial Times) <https://www.ft.com/content/dd007c3b-1d79-4835-bfae-ea568aec45b6?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Kevin Warsh comes to the Fed at a particularly difficult time. Trump wants rates to be cut “substantially” and “immediately", but war makes this unlikely. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/a11d75a8-5885-4d65-97c1-0cab85f7df4d?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Senate Democrats will question Warsh today after he disclosed that he owns $100m in assets last week but said the underlying details were confidential. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/economy/central-banking/kevin-warshs-finances-likely-to-play-key-role-in-confirmation-hearings-6dbde910?mod=hp_lead_pos3>
Nik Storonksy at Revolut says it will be two years before an IPO. (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-20/revolut-ceo-storonsky-says-digital-bank-s-ipo-is-two-years-out?srnd=homepage-americas>
The unhealthy habit of checking your phone, reading the news, and responding to emails and messages almost instantly endures far beyond the end of a banking career. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/98ab4190-9dbc-4f6c-bc3f-1a8afd0f4cc1>

item.title: "When you work for a bank, you're no more than a disposable number on a spreadsheet"
item.description:
Leon Chervinsky seems like the sort of person who could hold a lot of ire.
Chervinsky, who has a degree in electrical engineering from the New York Institute of Technology, spent two and a half decades doing the sorts of US banking jobs that can be a hard grind. He was running programs at the likes of Citi and Goldman Sachs. He was coaching leaders. He was pushing technological transformations.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Six years ago, Chervinsky was let go in some extremely difficult personal circumstances. He hasn't worked in financial services since. He's not bitter. Instead, the ever-avuncular Chervinsky has set himself a new task of warning other people in his old industry that career-death can come when you're least expecting it and when it seems least appropriate.
When it does, he wants you to know how to handle it.
Chervinsky's own career ended when he was a senior vice president (SVP). It was during COVID, six months after a close relative had narrowly survived death in a New York ICU. The US bank he was working for decided to move his function to a lower cost location. When he was unable to move because of his sick relative, he was dismissed. "I couldn't leave my family," he says. "My joke was that the commute would kill me but they said I didn't have to commute - I was fired. I'd been there for over a decade."
Chervinsky says his experience is a reminder that work isn't personal; it's procedural. "It's not just banks, it's all large organisations," he says. "They're looking for returns and if they can eliminate some jobs and save some dollars they will do that. You're no more than a number on a spreadsheet to them."
When he unearthed this reality, Chervinsky took three things before leaving his office forever: a paperweight given to him by a senior banker; his name from the door, and the photo of his children from the desk. With these in hand, he walked into a new life which has been more changeable than his corporate past. "I've worked in healthcare," says Chervinsky. "I've worked in cancer research."
He's ended up helping people like him, who leave long term corporate jobs with a sense of astonishment and loss. Six years out of banking, Chervinsky describes himself as an "identity coach:" he works to disentangle people from their jobs in a way that is becoming more necessary than ever. "People come to me and say that I was an MD in a bank and I'd like to do the same again," he tells us. "I say 'Buddy, those jobs aren't out there, and even if they are, they're likely to be very different to what you're used to.'"
If you lost your job unexpectedly, you need to be flexible, but before you can be flexible you will likely be lost and confused. Getting beyond this point means forging a whole new identity that has nothing to do with your job. "It is the most important work you have never been paid to do," reflects Chervinksy.
Identity building is hardest when you're already out of work. It's easiest when you can use your job as a crutch and can test other things alongside it. Chervinksy's experience is a reminder that it's never to late to make sure your job isn't your sole defining feature. "Many, many of my clients are senior managers who've been laid off," he tells us. "Jobs are disappearing and changing, both for junior developers and for senior people. I have one client who was managing 70 developers. Now she's managing 20 humans and 40 bots."

item.title: Citi's global head of AI joined in September and has just gone again
item.description:
Where is Shobhit Varshney? Not, it seems, at Citi. Which is curious, given that it wasn't long ago that he arrived.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Varshney was Citi's global head of AI. He arrived seven months ago from IBM and was lauded by Citi COO Anand Selva </news/citi-s-apparent-demotion-of-anand-selvakesari-is-not-what-it-seems-but-still-complicated>as an "incredible addition to the team" by virtue of his leadership, vision and "deep experience driving major AI transformations at large organizations."
Now Varshney is understood to have taken all those things on his way.
He didn't respond to a request to comment for this article. A spokesperson for Citi said: "Shobhit Varshney has decided to leave Citi for personal reasons. We thank him for his contributions and wish him well. Our talented team remains focused on driving the development and implementation of AI tools and solutions that drive business growth, improve client and colleague experience, reduce risk and improve operational efficiency."
Varshney spent 13 years at IBM and so is not in the habit of flitting around.
His disappearance comes as Citi prepares for an investor day in May when it's promised </news/citi-seems-to-be-cutting-more-expensive-people-as-data-transformation-remains-a-sticking-point> to talk at length about its plans for permeating AI throughout the organisation. New Citi CFO Gonzalo Luchetti said </news/banking-jobs-2026> earlier this month that the bank is likely to end 2026 with fewer people than it began, as structural efficiencies relating to the technology start to bite.

item.title: Rising hedge fund paying $1m in London hired an ex-Balyasny quant
item.description:
Ilex Capital, </news/when-an-ex-citadel-portfolio-manager-sets-up-a-new-fund-should-you-join> a hedge fund founded by former Citadel traders in London, has been rising fast. Its assets under management have swelled to $7bn <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2025-12-11/ex-citadel-duo-s-hedge-fund-ilex-grows-assets-to-7-billion>, its headcount has exceeded 40 and it has moved into a big new Kensington office. Among the recent arrivals are quants. </news/quant-jobs-finance>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This month, Ilex hired James Osoi as a quant researcher </news/how-to-get-a-quant-research-job-in-a-hedge-fund>. He was most recently at private equity firm Orbis Capital Management, where he worked on quant research and data insights, but he also spent over five years at Balyasny. There, he worked in quant and quantamental research across London and Singapore.
Ilex doesn't seem to have many quants, but one of its longest serving is Paul Ward. He joined last January from UBS, where he was head of quantitative investment solutions for UBS' wealth management arm.
Working at Ilex can be lucrative. In its most recent accounts for the year ending March 2025, the hedge fund said it paid 23 people an average of £738k ($997k) in 'salaries and wages'. The top earner at Ilex, meanwhile, brought home £80m. </news/bank-of-america-associate-paralympics#:~:text=The%20highest%20earner%20at%20Ilex%20Capital%20was%20paid%20%C2%A380m.%20(Financial%20News)%C2%A0>

item.title: A Japanese bank has lost many members of its credit trading team
item.description:
US banks have paid their bonuses </news/everything-we-know-about-2026-bonuses-by-bank> for 2025. So have European banks. Japanese banks, have not.
Insiders say that Mizuho, one such Japanese bank, usually announces its bonuses in April before paying them in May. This year, various traders have left before May arrives.
The most recent among them is Asif Godall, Mizuho's most senior trader in Europe. Financial News <https://www.fnlondon.com/articles/mizuhos-top-trader-in-europe-leaves-da994a32> reported last week that Godall has left the bank to "pursue other opportunities." Godall didn't respond to a request to comment on what these might be, but given his historic success at sniffing out wrongdoing <https://www.linkedin.com/feed/update/urn:li:activity:7435276734385790976/> at collapsed lender MFS while he was at Cairn Capital, private credit </news/private-credit>firms might want to hire him. Godall is understood to have left entirely amicably.
Mizuho isn't commenting on Godall's departure. Nor is it commenting on the disappearance of various members of Godall's team in the past six months. Co-heads of credit trading Hardeep Bariana and Aristo Vounakis </news/mizuho-s-head-of-credit-trading-had-a-nice-long-rest-and-then-suddenly-disappeared> left in March. Credit trader Mostafa Ismail left in December and is now at Barclays. Joseph Rock, another credit trader, went to UBS in November. Bader Barghoti, an emerging markets credit trader, went to rival Japanese bank SMBC in January. David Ceruti, another senior credit trader at Mizuho, is understood to be enroute to SMBC, too.
The implication is that Mizuho might need to hire some more credit traders, soon. It's not clear how much money it will have available to pay them: there are suggestions that some of the leavers were among its highest profit generators in the past.

item.title: "My quant team is falling apart because of my new manager and his fantasy KPIs"
item.description:
Quant finance </news/quant-jobs-finance>, in theory, is one of the most intellectually stimulating environments to work in. You're at the intersection of markets, math and coding </news/programming-languages-for-a-career-in-finance>, surrounded by talented, experienced people. This was the case for me until my team spectacularly collapsed under new management. </news/quants-promotions>
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
This manager joined and immediately began to restructure the team under the guise of separating the wheat from the chaff. Underperformers were let go and, once the manager's non-poaching period expired, they were replaced by his former subordinates. When he had enough of his own chess pieces on the board, the downfall began.
The team was separated in to sub-divisions led by people close to the manager. He allocated his people to these sub-teams not based on where they would do their best work, but where his people would benefit the most. Senior management refused to acknowledge how difficult the situation was for the rest of us and kept everything on a highly selective, need-to-know basis (which usually means management-needs-you-to-not-know).
Because of this, a lot of our best people have voted with their feet and left. Morale has plummeted unrecoverably, making more people want to leave. It's a vicious cycle.
The business rarely understands where the actual expertise lies with which quants, and has created a few fantasy KPIs despite all the internal chaos. These get even harder to achieve the more people leave. The workload on the remaining experienced team mates is soul crushing. I've been biding my time and interviewing </news/quant-interview-questions-finance> with other firms; soon I'll do my part to sink this ship even further.
Brock Newsom is a pseudonym

item.title: Hedge fund Balyasny has hired Macquarie's commodities technology guy
item.description:
It's not just commodities traders that leave Macquarie for hedge funds. Commodities technologists do too.
Peter Mill, the global head of commodities technology strategy and head of markets technology for EMEA at Macquarie, is joining Balynasy Asset Management as head of commodities technology according to his LinkedIn profile.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Mill, who didn't respond to a request to comment for this article, was based in London for Macquarie. It's not clear whether he will also be based in London with BAM.
Balyasny, like many multistrategy hedge funds, has been building out its commodities team. It hired Dan Deighton </news/goldman-sachs-to-balyasny>from Goldman Sachs as head of global commodities in 2022. Deighton hired various other ex-Goldman traders, including star Goldman Sachs researcher Damien Courvalin </news/goldman-sachs-to-balyasny> who mysteriously disappeared in March.
Courvalin was based in New York, as is Deighton. Mill may yet migrate.
Macquarie commodities trading business was historically one of the most successful on the sell-side. However, Nick O'Kane </news/Nick-o-kane-macquarie-trader-resigned>, the bank's top oil trader, left in 2024 for Mercuria after what Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2025-11-03/australian-bank-macquarie-loses-oil-traders-as-it-cuts-back-on-risk>subsequently described as a "reappraisal" of risk management appetite at the bank. Net profit from commodities trading fell 19% last year as a result.
Commodities trading technology is a specialist area and can be complex given the breadth of commodities traded. Physical commodities trading creates another layer of complexity with the need for physical scheduling systems. Balyasny began building <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2024-05-07/balyasny-building-physical-natural-gas-power-business-in-europe?utm_source=website&utm_medium=share&utm_campaign=linkedin> a physical natural gas and power trading business in Europe in 2024.

item.title: Morning Coffee: Hedge fund gardening leave and the $100m+ job offer. Deutsche Bank's richest ex-trader passed over by Google
item.description:
It's a good time to go on gardening leave. Summer is coming. The on-off war is still on. And maybe you will get a bigger, better, offer while you're on the beach.
💥Follow us on WhatsApp for news alerts. <https://whatsapp.com/channel/0029VacsPmzKGGGK91C7dR39>💥
Bloomberg <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/features/2026-04-19/hedge-funds-frenzied-job-market-sends-pay-spiraling-higher>says the latter is happening to the most desired portfolio managers </news/finance/portfolio-manager-job-hedge-fund-lifestyle> in hedge funds. They leave for gardening leave with one job lined up, and then another fund jumps in with a bigger and better offer and hires them during their time out of the market.
It happens a lot. Bloomberg counts 14 hedge fund professionals in the recent past who've had their eyes turned by such things. They include Stanley Sheriff </news/32-year-old-star-ex-goldman-sachs-trader-poached-by-hedge-fund-balyasny-one-week-into-job>, the 32-year-old ex-Goldman Sachs rates trader who briefly went to Taula Capital Management in May 2024, but who left Taula for Balyasny Asset Management within a month. More recently, they include Tarun Tyagi, who left Capula for Millennium a year ago, only to bought back by Capula shortly before he was due to join Millennium as his year's gardening leave ended. Capula even repaid Tyagi the money Millennium gave him to sit out, and so he effectively achieved a very long paid holiday with his current employer.
This technique, known as the "interception trade" can be used to supercharge compensation. Bloomberg points to $120m hedge fund pay packets, and the possibility of achieving higher offers if you're on the way to one hedge fund when another one intercepts waving more cash. Hedge fund professionals are "economic animals", says one observer. They like this kind of thing.
Hedge fund headhunters like it less. Jason Kennedy, a hedge fund headhunter who has his very own family office, says he spent four years finding the perfect job for a trader, who duly sat out of the market in preparation for joining it, but was diverted to another fund for four times the money just before he was due to arrive. Kennedy lost his fee. The trader reportedly promised to cover Kennedy's cost, before ignoring his calls.
It doesn't help that as traders' mandatory garden leave comes to an end, they become increasingly desirable and increasingly valuable to all the other funds that want plug-and-play candidates. With this in mind, some headhunters are reportedly assembling lists of candidates enroute from one fund to another so that they can charge in and gazzump them. In doing so, they're rendering their own lives more stressful. Headhunters presumably now have to spend the entirety of their own candidates' gardening leave trying to persuade their "economic animals" not to let them down. Good luck with that.
Separately, at this stage of humanity, the most illustrious Deutsche Bank alumnus is not Josef Ackermann, the hirsute ex-Deutsche CEO, but Alexander Gerko, the bearded ex-trader.
Gerko, who now runs market making firm XTX </news/difference-between-electronic-trading-firms>, will imminently receive a £1.5bn dividend as XTX's majority shareholder, says Bloomberg. <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-17/algo-trader-shares-3-billion-payday-amid-xtx-earnings-rise?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260417&utm_campaign=author_19241437>Remaining XTX shareholders will share £700m between them.
Alongside his activities at XTX, Gerko also has a frank and sometimes profane presence on social media where he is known for blocking people and expressing large opinions. There, he recently also divulged an unknown element of his backstory: before XTX and before even Deutsche Bank, Gerko said he worked for Dulance, a price comparison engine in Russia. Dulance was bought by Google, and Gerko says he pitched his idea for XTX to Peter Norvig, Google's then-director of research. Gerko says Norvig reportedly turned him down because finance was "evil". At that stage Google was still "good." It's not clear where XTX is on the scale.
Meanwhile...
It sounds like Citadel Securities will want to hire prediction markets specialists soon, but not for sports contracts. “We’re not there yet. There is not that much liquidity,” says president Jim Esposito. As markets "ramp and scale", the firm will "potentially get involved.” (Bloomberg) <https://www.bloomberg.com/news/articles/2026-04-16/citadel-securities-looks-at-prediction-markets-as-trading-scales?utm_medium=email&utm_source=author_alert&utm_term=260417&utm_campaign=author_19908303>
Flow Traders is the poor relation in electronic trading. Only 15% of its revenues are generated in the Americas and it hasn't grown there in four years. Two-thirds of its 660 people are in Amsterdam. (Rupak Ghose) <https://rupakghose.substack.com/p/the-traders-of-amsterdam?utm_source=substack&publication_id=1898843&post_id=194501275&utm_medium=email&utm_content=share&utm_campaign=email-share&triggerShare=true&isFreemail=true&r=qrn8&triedRedirect=true>
Saudi Arabia’s Public Investment Fund has $900bn in assets but has been making some large investments ($55bn for Electronic Arts, $10bn towards Paramount, $100bn for AI) and is maybe spent out. (Bloomberg) <https://www.wsj.com/world/middle-east/saudi-fund-behind-liv-golf-has-900-billion-but-is-still-strapped-for-cash-15acaeac?mod=e2tw>
Mohammed Al-Jadaan, Saudi Arabia’s finance minister: "I still believe that markets and all other people are still trying to take a more positive picture than what reality is. Anyone who’s counting for a quick recovery, even if there is a total end of hostilities, will need to recalculate that.” (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/economy/global/iran-war-casts-gloom-over-outlook-of-world-finance-ministers-and-investors-df15aaae?mod=panda_wsj_author_alert>
Doug Ostrover and Marc Lipschultz are no longer using their shares in Blue Owl as collateral for large personal loans. (FT) <https://www.ft.com/content/a90dce4c-764c-4b72-8411-685479f46eed?syn-25a6b1a6=1>
Hedge funds own a record-high 8% of US Treasuries, and with combined repo and prime brokerage borrowing exceeding $6 trillion, any forced unwind of these leveraged positions could send shockwaves through global fixed income markets. (Apollo) <https://www.apollo.com/wealth/the-daily-spark/hedge-fund-leverage-in-treasuries-record-high>
If it drizzles when you visit a college, you won't want to go there. It's not clear whether this also applies to hedge fund jobs. (WSJ) <https://www.wsj.com/us-news/education/the-hidden-factor-in-choosing-a-college-did-it-rain-on-your-tour-8acbd946?mod=e2tw>
“if you need to work to maintain your lifestyle, you are not a member of the upper class.” (The Purse) <https://www.thepurse.co/whats-the-deal-with-the-middle-class/?ref=the-purse-newsletter>
